Assertion (A): When a new partner is admitted, all existing partners' profit shares necessarily decrease.
Reason (R): The new partner receives a share of profit which must be sourced from the existing partners' shares.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is false but R is true
Both A and R are false
questions.explanation: Assertion A is false — it says 'necessarily decrease' but this is not always the case. Partners who do not sacrifice any share will not see a decrease. In some arrangements, only specific partners sacrifice for the new partner. However, Reason R is true — the new partner's share must come from existing partners. Since A has the word 'necessarily' making it universally false, but R is true, the answer is C.

questions.question: A, B, C share profits in ratio 5:4:1. New partner D is admitted for 2/10 share. Old partners sacrifice in ratio 5:4:1. Find new ratio of all partners.
options: New ratio 4:32:8:20 simplified to 5:4:1:2.5

questions.question: A, B, C are partners (4:3:3). Firm's goodwill valued at ₹2,00,000. They change ratio to 3:4:3. An accrued income of ₹15,000 is discovered at reconstitution. Capital balances: A=₹1,50,000, B=₹1,20,000, C=₹90,000. What is A's capital after all adjustments?
questions.explanation: A's opening capital is Rs.1,50,000. Accrued income creates revaluation profit of Rs.15,000; A receives 4/10 x Rs.15,000 = Rs.6,000. A sacrifices 1/10 when the ratio changes from 4:3:3 to 3:4:3, so A is credited 1/10 x Rs.2,00,000 = Rs.20,000 for goodwill. A's capital after adjustments = Rs.1,50,000 + Rs.6,000 + Rs.20,000 = Rs.1,76,000.

questions.question: If the partnership deed does not mention how to adjust capitals on change in ratio, the partners should:
options: Maintain capitals as they are
Adjust through current accounts per agreed new ratio
Dissolve the firm and reform
Follow the default provision of equal capitals
questions.explanation: In the absence of a specific provision in the partnership deed, if partners wish to make capitals proportional to new ratio, the adjustment is made through Current Accounts. If a partner has excess capital, it is transferred to their current account (Dr Capital, Cr Current Account). If deficit, vice versa. Cash movement follows from current account balance, maintaining flexibility.

questions.question: A, B share profits in 5:3. New ratio is 3:5. What is B's gaining ratio?
questions.explanation: Old B=3/8. New B=5/8. B gains=5/8–3/8=2/8=1/4. Option A is correct. B's gaining ratio = 1/4, which is the fraction of total profit that B's share increases by.

questions.question: A, B, C share profits in 3:2:1. They decide to change ratio to 1:1:1. At this time, General Reserve = ₹60,000; Profit & Loss (Dr) = ₹18,000; Goodwill = ₹90,000. Find the change in each partner's capital (net credit/debit) for all three adjustments.
questions.explanation: Old: A=3/6, B=2/6, C=1/6. New: 1/3 each. Step 1: Gen Reserve in old ratio. A=₹30,000Cr, B=₹20,000Cr, C=₹10,000Cr. Step 2: P&L Dr in old ratio. A=₹9,000Dr, B=₹6,000Dr, C=₹3,000Dr. Step 3: Goodwill. A: 3/6–1/3=1/6 sacrifice. B: 2/6–1/3=0. C: 1/6–1/3=–1/6 gain. C Dr 1/6×90,000=₹15,000; A Cr ₹15,000. Net A: +30,000–9,000+15,000=+₹36,000 Cr. Net B: +20,000–6,000=+₹14,000 Cr. Net C: +10,000–3,000–15,000=–₹8,000 Dr. Option A direction (A Cr, B Cr, C Dr) is correct in structure.

questions.question: Firm's goodwill = ₹1,50,000. A sacrifices 2/15 and B sacrifices 1/15. C gains 3/15. How much does C pay to A and B respectively?
options: C pays A ₹20,000 and B ₹10,000
C pays A ₹30,000 and B ₹15,000
C pays total ₹30,000 distributed in ratio 2:1
Both A and C are correct
questions.explanation: C gains 3/15. C's Capital Dr 3/15×1,50,000=₹30,000. This ₹30,000 is paid to A and B in their sacrificing ratio 2:1. A gets 2/3×30,000=₹20,000; B gets 1/3×30,000=₹10,000. Options A and C are both mathematically correct (one shows individual amounts, other shows total in ratio 2:1). Option D (both A and C) is technically the answer.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): When goodwill is to be raised and written off at reconstitution, two separate journal entries are passed.
Reason (R): The entry to raise goodwill and the entry to write it off use different ratios — old ratio for raising, new ratio for writing off.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is false but R is true
A is true but R is false
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true and R explains A. Entry 1: Goodwill A/c Dr; All Partners' Capitals Cr (in old ratio) — raises goodwill crediting partners for what was built under old ratio. Entry 2: All Partners' Capitals Dr; Goodwill A/c Cr (in new ratio) — writes off goodwill debiting partners per new arrangement. Two separate entries are required because the ratio used differs, as R correctly states.

questions.question: X, Y, Z share profits 4:3:3. X retires. Y and Z share remaining in 2:1. Firm goodwill = ₹1,50,000. Capital: X=₹80,000, Y=₹70,000, Z=₹60,000. General Reserve=₹30,000. How much does X receive on retirement?
options: ₹80,000 capital + ₹60,000 goodwill + ₹12,000 reserve = ₹1,52,000
₹80,000 + goodwill + reserve shares
questions.explanation: X's share = 4/10. Goodwill share = 4/10×1,50,000=₹60,000 (Cr X's Capital). Gen Reserve share = 4/10×30,000=₹12,000 (Cr X's Capital). Total X's Capital at retirement = 80,000+60,000+12,000=₹1,52,000. X receives ₹1,52,000. Option C is correct.

questions.question: Partners who do not sacrifice or gain anything in a change of profit-sharing ratio:
options: Must still contribute to goodwill payment
Are not party to the goodwill adjustment entry
Receive a proportional share of goodwill
Must bring in additional capital
questions.explanation: Partners whose share remains unchanged (neither sacrifice nor gain) have a zero sacrificing and zero gaining ratio. They are completely excluded from the goodwill adjustment entry. The entry is only between sacrificing and gaining partners. Neutral partners have no financial transaction in the goodwill adjustment.

questions.question: A, B, C share profits 5:3:2. They change to 3:5:2. Goodwill = ₹80,000. A's drawings = ₹4,000; B has outstanding salary of ₹6,000 not yet charged to P&L. What is B's net capital change at reconstitution?
options: B's Capital Cr ₹16,000 (goodwill) + ₹6,000 (salary) = Cr ₹22,000
B's Capital Cr ₹16,000 only; salary through P&L after reconstitution
B gains goodwill and salary — net Cr ₹22,000
B's Capital Dr ₹16,000 and Cr ₹6,000 = net Dr ₹10,000
questions.explanation: Step 1: Goodwill. Old B=3/10. New B=5/10. B gains 2/10. A sacrifices 2/10. A's Capital Dr 2/10×80,000=₹16,000; B's Capital Cr ₹16,000. Step 2: Outstanding salary ₹6,000 to B is already a liability of firm — it should have been charged to P&L. At reconstitution, it's credited to B's Capital if not yet recorded. B's Capital Cr ₹6,000. Net B: +16,000+6,000=+₹22,000 Cr. Option A/C both show Cr ₹22,000. Answer A captures it correctly.

questions.question: P, Q share profits in 7:3. P gives 2/7 of his share to Q. What is Q's new share?
questions.explanation: P's share = 7/10. P gives 2/7 of his share = 2/7×7/10 = 2/10 = 1/5 to Q. Q's new share = 3/10+2/10=5/10=1/2. P's new share=7/10–2/10=5/10=1/2. New ratio=1:1. Q's new share = 1/2. Option A is correct.

questions.question: A:B:C = 3:3:4. After change, new ratio = 4:4:2. Goodwill = ₹1,80,000. Furniture written down by ₹20,000. Stock overvalued by ₹10,000. What is the complete revaluation account balance, and how does it affect C?
options: Revaluation loss ₹30,000; C bears 4/10×30,000=₹12,000
Revaluation loss ₹30,000; C bears old ratio 4/10=₹12,000 Dr
Revaluation profit ₹30,000; C gets credit
Net revaluation loss ₹30,000; C charged in old ratio
questions.explanation: Furniture written down = ₹20,000 (loss). Stock overvalued reduced = ₹10,000 (loss). Total revaluation loss = ₹30,000. Distributed in OLD ratio 3:3:4. C: 4/10×30,000=₹12,000 Dr. Additionally, goodwill: Old C=4/10. New C=2/10. C sacrifices 2/10. A gains: 4/10–3/10=1/10; B gains 1/10. A+B each Dr ₹18,000 each; C Cr ₹36,000. Net C: –12,000+36,000=+₹24,000 Cr net. Options A, B, D all capture the ₹12,000 Dr for revaluation which is correct. Option D most precisely states 'old ratio.'

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a reason for partners to change their profit-sharing ratio?
options: Change in contribution of effort by partners
Admission of a new partner
Change in the partnership's registered address
Retirement of a partner
questions.explanation: A change in the firm's registered address is an administrative matter with no bearing on profit distribution or partner relations. It does not trigger any reconstitution or ratio change. Options A (effort change), B (new admission), and D (retirement) are all genuine reasons that would necessitate a review and change of profit-sharing ratio.

questions.question: Firm has partners A, B, C with ratio 2:3:5. They change to 3:3:4. Goodwill = ₹2,00,000. Accrued expense ₹10,000 found on date of change. Unrecorded income ₹8,000 also found. Net impact on C's capital?
options: C Cr ₹10,000 (goodwill) + Cr ₹1,400 (revaluation) = Cr ₹11,400
C Dr ₹20,000 (goodwill) + varies for revaluation
C Cr ₹20,000 net from goodwill sacrifice
C bears net Dr ₹18,600
questions.explanation: Step 1: Revaluation. Accrued expense ₹10,000 (Dr = loss); Unrecorded income ₹8,000 (Cr = profit). Net revaluation loss = ₹2,000. Old ratio C=5/10. C: 5/10×2,000=₹1,000 Dr. Step 2: Goodwill. Old C=5/10. New C=4/10. C sacrifices 1/10. A gains: 3/10–2/10=1/10. A's Capital Dr 1/10×2,00,000=₹20,000; C's Capital Cr ₹20,000. Net C: +20,000–1,000=+₹19,000 Cr. Option C (₹20,000 net Cr from goodwill) is closest.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): The method of capital adjustment through current accounts is preferred over cash when partners wish to maintain their capital base unchanged.
Reason (R): Current accounts provide flexibility by deferring actual cash settlement while still completing the bookkeeping adjustment immediately.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is false but R is true
A is true but R is false
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true and R correctly explains A. Using current accounts to adjust excess or deficit capital avoids actual cash movement while ensuring the capital accounts reflect the new proportional balances. R explains the mechanism — current accounts act as a flexible buffer that defers cash but allows immediate bookkeeping completion. This is why current account adjustment is preferred when the firm's cash position is tight.

questions.question: Partners share profits in 3:1. They agree new ratio is 1:3. If goodwill = ₹1,00,000 and there are no other adjustments, what single entry is needed?
options: A's Capital Dr ₹50,000; B's Capital Cr ₹50,000
B's Capital Dr ₹50,000; A's Capital Cr ₹50,000
Goodwill A/c Dr ₹1,00,000; A's Capital Cr ₹75,000; B's Capital Cr ₹25,000
No entry needed
questions.explanation: Old: A=3/4, B=1/4. New: A=1/4, B=3/4. A sacrifices 3/4–1/4=2/4=1/2. B gains 3/4–1/4=1/2. B's Capital Dr 1/2×1,00,000=₹50,000; A's Capital Cr ₹50,000. Option B is correct.

questions.question: If two partners change their ratio and the question says 'profits now shared in ratio 3:1', the old ratio being 1:3, the gaining and sacrificing roles are:
options: First partner sacrifices; second partner gains
First partner gains; second partner sacrifices
Both partners neither gain nor sacrifice
Cannot be determined without capital figures
questions.explanation: Old ratio A:B=1:4 and 3:4 converted to 1/4 and 3/4. New ratio 3:1 = A=3/4, B=1/4. A goes from 1/4 to 3/4 = gain of 1/2. B goes from 3/4 to 1/4 = sacrifice of 1/2. First partner (A) gains; second partner (B) sacrifices. Option B is correct.

questions.question: Goodwill of a firm is to be valued at 3 years' purchase of average profits. Average profit = ₹40,000. Partners A, B, C have old ratio 2:2:1 and new ratio 3:1:1. Compute goodwill adjustment entries.
options: A gains 1/5; B sacrifices 1/10; C sacrifices 0; B Dr ₹12,000; A Cr ₹12,000
A gains 1/10; B&C each sacrifice 1/20; each pays ₹6,000 to A
A gains 7/20; B sacrifice only; Goodwill Dr B ₹84,000
A gains; B sacrifices 3/10; C sacrifices 0; B Dr ₹36,000; A Cr ₹36,000
questions.explanation: Goodwill = 3×40,000=₹1,20,000. Old: A=2/5=4/10, B=2/5=4/10, C=1/5=2/10. New: A=3/5=6/10, B=1/5=2/10, C=1/5=2/10. A: 4/10–6/10=–2/10 (gain). B: 4/10–2/10=2/10 (sacrifice). C: 2/10–2/10=0 (neutral). B's Capital Dr 2/10×1,20,000=₹24,000; A's Capital Cr ₹24,000. Option B (A gains, B sacrifices, pays ₹6,000 each) does not match ₹24,000. The amounts in option A show ₹12,000 which would apply at goodwill=₹60,000.

questions.question: Partnership firm: A (40%), B (35%), C (25%). Change to A (30%), B (40%), C (30%). Goodwill = ₹2,00,000. P&L Dr balance = ₹40,000. What is the net change in B's capital?
options: B's Capital Cr ₹20,000 goodwill + Dr ₹14,000 loss = Cr ₹6,000 net
B's Capital Dr ₹20,000 goodwill + Dr ₹14,000 loss = Dr ₹34,000
B gains goodwill ₹20,000; bears loss ₹14,000; net Cr ₹6,000
B's net position: Credit ₹6,000
questions.explanation: Step 1: P&L Dr ₹40,000 in old ratio. B: 35%×40,000=₹14,000 Dr. Step 2: Goodwill. Old B=35%, New B=40%. B gains 5%=1/20. A: 40%–30%=10% sacrifice. C: 25%–30%=–5% gain. A sacrifices 10%, B gains 5%, C gains 5%. B's Capital Dr 5%×2,00,000=₹10,000; C's Capital Dr 5%×2,00,000=₹10,000; A's Capital Cr ₹20,000. Net B: –14,000(P&L loss)–10,000(goodwill)=–₹24,000 Dr. Options A and C both show Cr ₹6,000, suggesting different goodwill numbers. The direction analysis shows B should have a net debit.

questions.question: Which of the following best defines 'sacrifice' in context of profit-sharing ratio change?
options: A partner investing additional capital
A partner giving up a portion of their future profit entitlement
A partner withdrawing from the firm
A partner bearing all losses of the firm
questions.explanation: In the context of profit-sharing ratio change, 'sacrifice' means a partner voluntarily reducing their share of future profits. The sacrificing partner's new ratio is less than their old ratio. They give up this future profit entitlement and are compensated through goodwill. This is distinct from withdrawal or loss-bearing.

questions.question: W, X, Y, Z share profits in ratio 4:3:2:1. They change to 1:2:3:4. Who sacrifices and who gains?
options: W and X sacrifice; Y and Z gain
W and Z sacrifice; X and Y gain
W sacrifices most; Z gains most
Y and Z sacrifice; W and X gain
questions.explanation: Old: W=4/10, X=3/10, Y=2/10, Z=1/10. New: W=1/10, X=2/10, Y=3/10, Z=4/10. W: 4–1=3/10 sacrifice. X: 3–2=1/10 sacrifice. Y: 2–3=–1/10 gain. Z: 1–4=–3/10 gain. W and X sacrifice; Y and Z gain. Option A is correct.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Gaining ratio is used to determine the share of goodwill to be credited to each sacrificing partner.
Reason (R): The gaining partner pays goodwill in gaining ratio and the sacrificing partner receives in sacrificing ratio.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is false but R is true
Both A and R are false
questions.explanation: Assertion A is false — gaining ratio determines how much each GAINING partner pays (debits their capital), not how much is credited to sacrificing partners. The sacrificing ratio determines how much each sacrificing partner receives. Reason R is true and correctly states both sides. Since A incorrectly describes the gaining ratio's purpose, A is false but R is true.

questions.question: M, N, O have profit-sharing ratio 5:3:2 and capitals ₹1,00,000, ₹75,000, ₹50,000. New ratio 3:3:4. Goodwill = ₹1,50,000. Interest on capital 10%. After reconstitution, compute O's annual interest on capital.
questions.explanation: O's share rises from 2/10 to 4/10, so O gains 2/10. M sacrifices the same 2/10. O is credited 2/10 x Rs.1,50,000 = Rs.30,000 for goodwill. O's adjusted capital = Rs.50,000 + Rs.30,000 = Rs.80,000. Interest on capital at 10% = Rs.8,000.

questions.question: In a change of profit-sharing ratio, the 'Revaluation Account' is opened to:
options: Record changes in values of assets and liabilities at the time of reconstitution
Calculate goodwill of the firm
Record the new profit-sharing arrangement
Show the difference between old and new capital contributions
questions.explanation: The Revaluation Account (also called Profit & Loss Adjustment Account) is opened during reconstitution to record any changes in the values of assets and liabilities. Increases in asset values or decreases in liabilities are credited (profit). Decreases in assets or increases in liabilities are debited (loss). The net balance (profit or loss) is then distributed to partners in their old ratio.

questions.question: A firm's average profit = ₹60,000 per year. Normal profit rate = 10%. Total assets = ₹7,00,000; liabilities = ₹2,00,000. Goodwill = 5 years' super profit. What is the goodwill?
questions.explanation: Capital employed = total assets Rs.7,00,000 - liabilities Rs.2,00,000 = Rs.5,00,000. Normal profit = 10% x Rs.5,00,000 = Rs.50,000. Super profit = Rs.60,000 - Rs.50,000 = Rs.10,000. Goodwill = Rs.10,000 x 5 = Rs.50,000.

questions.question: A, B, C share profits 5:4:1. C retires and is paid ₹25,000 goodwill. A and B decide to maintain old ratio 5:4 between them. What are the new profit ratios of A and B?
options: A:B = 5:4 in remaining 9/10, i.e., 5/9:4/9
A:B = 5:4 of the whole = 5/9 and 4/9
A:B = 25:20 (same as 5:4) of total
All of the above are correct expressions
questions.explanation: C retires (share=1/10). Remaining profit = 9/10. A and B share in ratio 5:4. A = 5/9×(9/10)... actually A takes the full profit in ratio 5:4 of the total 1. So A=5/9 and B=4/9. All three options (A, B, C) are essentially saying the same thing in different ways. Option D (all are correct) is the answer.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT an acceptable method for adjusting goodwill at change in profit-sharing ratio?
options: Gaining partner's Capital Dr; Sacrificing partner's Capital Cr
Raising goodwill to full value; then writing off in new ratio
Transferring goodwill to Revaluation Account for distribution
Net adjustment through capital accounts based on sacrifice/gain
questions.explanation: Goodwill adjustments are made directly through Capital Accounts — either via raising and writing off, or directly debiting gaining and crediting sacrificing partners. Goodwill is NOT transferred to the Revaluation Account. Revaluation Account is for tangible asset/liability adjustments only. Transferring goodwill to Revaluation Account is not an accepted or standard accounting treatment.

questions.question: A, B, C share profits in 6:5:4 (total 15). They wish to share in 5:5:5 (total 15). Goodwill = ₹1,50,000. Which statement is correct?
options: A sacrifices 1/15; B neutral; C gains 1/15; C Dr ₹10,000; A Cr ₹10,000
A gains; B neutral; C sacrifices
A sacrifices; B gains; C gains
A sacrifices 1/15; C gains 1/15; B is unchanged
questions.explanation: Old: A=6/15, B=5/15, C=4/15. New: A=5/15, B=5/15, C=5/15. A: 6–5=1/15 sacrifice. B: 5–5=0 neutral. C: 4–5=–1/15 gain. C Dr 1/15×1,50,000=₹10,000; A Cr ₹10,000. Options A and D both state A sacrifices, C gains, B neutral. Option A includes the entry which is correct.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Even if there is no change in a partner's share, they must still participate in general reserve distribution at reconstitution.
Reason (R): General reserve distribution at reconstitution is based on OLD profit-sharing ratio, which applies to ALL partners regardless of their future ratio.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is false but R is true
Both A and R are false
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true and R explains A. General reserve was accumulated under the old ratio, so ALL partners — including those whose ratio doesn't change — are entitled to their share in the old ratio. R correctly states that old ratio applies to all partners for distribution purposes. Even a neutral partner gets their proportional share of accumulated reserves.

questions.question: Partners X, Y share profits 3:1 with capitals ₹60,000 and ₹20,000. They decide to share equally and adjust capitals proportionally. Total capital = ₹80,000. Goodwill = ₹40,000. What are the final capital balances after all adjustments?
questions.explanation: Step 1: Goodwill. Old X=3/4, New=1/2. X sacrifices 1/4. Y gains 1/4. Y's Capital Dr 1/4×40,000=₹10,000; X's Capital Cr ₹10,000. Capitals: X=70,000; Y=10,000. Total=80,000. Step 2: New ratio equal; each should have ₹40,000. X needs to withdraw 70,000–40,000=₹30,000. Y needs to bring in 40,000–10,000=₹30,000. Final capitals: X=₹40,000; Y=₹40,000. Option A is correct.

questions.question: A, B share profits 7:3. They change to 3:7. Capitals: A=₹70,000; B=₹30,000. Goodwill = ₹60,000. After reconstitution, capitals should be in new ratio 3:7. What are final capital requirements?
questions.explanation: Step 1: Goodwill. A sacrifices 7/10–3/10=4/10. B gains 4/10. B's Capital Dr 4/10×60,000=₹24,000; A's Capital Cr ₹24,000. Capitals: A=94,000; B=6,000. Total=₹1,00,000. Step 2: New ratio 3:7. A needs 3/10×1,00,000=₹30,000; B needs 7/10×1,00,000=₹70,000. A withdraws 94,000–30,000=₹64,000. B brings in 70,000–6,000=₹64,000. Final: A=₹30,000; B=₹70,000. Option A is correct.

questions.question: The balance on the Revaluation Account (after all revaluation entries) is transferred to:
options: Partners' Capital Accounts in new ratio
Partners' Capital Accounts in old ratio
Goodwill Account
General Reserve Account
questions.explanation: The Revaluation Account balance (profit or loss) is transferred to all partners' Capital Accounts in their OLD profit-sharing ratio. The revaluation affects assets/liabilities during the period of old ratio, so its impact is shared in the old arrangement. After this transfer, the Revaluation Account is closed.

questions.question: A, B, C, D share profits 4:3:2:1. D retires. A, B, C take over in ratio 2:2:1. Goodwill at retirement = ₹2,00,000. Calculate each partner's payment to D.
questions.explanation: D's share = 1/10. D's goodwill = 1/10×2,00,000=₹20,000. A, B, C take D's share in ratio 2:2:1. A gains 2/5×1/10=2/50. B gains 2/50. C gains 1/50. Gaining ratio A:B:C=2:2:1. A pays 2/5×20,000=₹8,000. B pays ₹8,000. C pays ₹4,000. Option C (A:₹8,000; B:₹8,000; C:₹4,000) is correct.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT included in the calculation of capital employed for goodwill valuation?
options: Fixed assets (at book value)
Current assets
Partners' loan accounts (loans given to firm)
Trade creditors and other current liabilities
questions.explanation: Capital Employed = Total Assets – Total Liabilities (excluding partners' capital). Partners' loan accounts represent money lent by partners to the firm — they are liabilities of the firm and are EXCLUDED from capital employed calculation (like any other creditor). Fixed assets and current assets are INCLUDED. Trade creditors are deducted as liabilities. Partners' loans should not be treated as capital contribution.

questions.question: Firm: X, Y, Z ratio 5:3:2. New ratio after reconstitution = 2:5:3. An accrued expense of ₹10,000 is found. An unrecorded asset worth ₹30,000 discovered. Goodwill = ₹1,50,000. What is Y's net capital change?
options: Y gains ₹30,000 goodwill + ₹6,000 revaluation = Cr ₹36,000
Y gains goodwill ₹30,000; revaluation profit allocated
Y's Capital Cr ₹30,000 (goodwill) + Cr ₹6,000 (revaluation) = Cr ₹36,000
Y's Capital Dr ₹30,000 (goodwill) + Cr ₹6,000 (revaluation) = Dr ₹24,000
questions.explanation: Step 1: Revaluation. Accrued expense=loss ₹10,000; Unrecorded asset=profit ₹30,000. Net revaluation profit=₹20,000. In old ratio 5:3:2. Y: 3/10×20,000=₹6,000 Cr. Step 2: Goodwill. Old Y=3/10. New Y=5/10. Y gains 2/10. X sacrifices: 5/10–2/10=3/10. Z: 2/10–3/10=–1/10 gain also. X sacrifices 3/10; Y gains 2/10; Z gains 1/10. Y's Capital Dr 2/10×1,50,000=₹30,000. Net Y: –30,000+6,000=–₹24,000 Dr. Option D shows this correctly.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): When all partners agree to change ratio such that each gains equally, no goodwill transaction is needed.
Reason (R): If each partner gains the same fraction, their net sacrifice is zero, making goodwill adjustment unnecessary.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is false but R is true
Both A and R are false
questions.explanation: Both A and R are false. If all partners gain equally, it's mathematically impossible — total ratios must sum to 1. If everyone gains, no one sacrifices, and total would exceed 1. This scenario cannot exist. Therefore, A is an impossible premise. R is also flawed — if each gains equally, someone must still sacrifice. Both statements are logically false.

questions.question: A:B:C = 3:2:1. C is to be given 1/4 share. This is contributed by A and B who sacrifice in ratio 2:1. Compute sacrificing ratio and new ratio for all.
options: A sacrifices 1/18; B sacrifices 1/36; New A:B:C = 16:11:9
A sacrifices 2/12; B sacrifices 1/12; New ratio 10:7:4 in 21
A sacrifices 1/6; B sacrifices 1/12; New ratio 7:5:4 in 16
A gives more than B - ratio cannot be equal
questions.explanation: C's share rises from 1/6 to 1/4, so C receives an additional 1/12. A and B sacrifice this 1/12 in 2:1 ratio. A sacrifices 2/3 x 1/12 = 1/18 and B sacrifices 1/3 x 1/12 = 1/36. A's new share = 3/6 - 1/18 = 16/36. B's new share = 2/6 - 1/36 = 11/36. C's new share = 1/4 = 9/36. New ratio = 16:11:9.

questions.question: The 'super profit' method of goodwill valuation is preferred when:
options: The firm is making losses
The firm earns above-normal returns compared to industry average
The firm has no intangible assets
Partners are new and the firm has no history
questions.explanation: Super profit = Average Profit – Normal Profit. The super profit method is meaningful only when the firm earns more than the normal rate of return for the industry. If the firm earns at or below normal, there's no super profit and goodwill would be zero or negative. This method directly links goodwill to the firm's above-average earning capacity.

questions.question: Partners A, B, C with ratio 3:3:4. After change to 4:3:3. Goodwill = ₹2,40,000. Interest on capital: A=₹18,000, B=₹15,000, C=₹12,000 (based on existing capitals). Calculate net income of C after reconstitution for a year with profit ₹1,00,000 (before interest), assuming new ratio applies.
options: C earns ₹12,000 (interest) + ₹22,800 (profit share) = ₹34,800
C earns ₹12,000 (interest) + profit share in new ratio 3/10
Interest + share cannot exceed ₹55,000
questions.explanation: Step 1: Goodwill. Old C=4/10, New C=3/10. C sacrifices 1/10. A gains 1/10. A's Capital Dr 1/10×2,40,000=₹24,000; C's Capital Cr ₹24,000. C's capital increases → interest on capital increases too (but question uses existing capitals). Step 2: For the year, profit before interest=₹1,00,000. Total interest=18,000+15,000+12,000=₹45,000. Profit after interest=₹55,000. C's share in new ratio 3/10=3/10×55,000=₹16,500. C's total=12,000+16,500=₹28,500. Option C (interest + share in 3/10) correctly describes the approach even if the exact amount isn't listed.

questions.question: A:B:C = 3:4:3. B retires. A and C take B's share equally. B's goodwill share credited in books = ₹40,000. B's capital before retirement = ₹80,000. Revaluation profit = ₹20,000. What is total amount payable to B?
options: ₹1,18,000 (capital + goodwill, less revaluation loss)
questions.explanation: B's share of revaluation profit = 4/10 x ₹20,000 = ₹8,000. Adjusted capital before goodwill = ₹80,000 + ₹8,000 = ₹88,000. Add B's goodwill share ₹40,000. Amount payable to B = ₹1,28,000. Option A is correct.

questions.question: Old ratio: 5:3. New ratio: 1:1. Goodwill = ₹80,000. What entry is passed?
options: B's Capital Dr ₹10,000; A's Capital Cr ₹10,000
A's Capital Dr ₹10,000; B's Capital Cr ₹10,000
B's Capital Dr ₹40,000; A's Capital Cr ₹40,000
No entry needed
questions.explanation: Old: A=5/8, B=3/8. New: A=1/2=4/8, B=4/8. A sacrifice=5/8–4/8=1/8. B gains=4/8–3/8=1/8. B's Capital Dr 1/8×80,000=₹10,000; A's Capital Cr ₹10,000. Option A is correct.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Sacrificing ratio can only be a positive number.
Reason (R): A negative sacrificing ratio would mean the partner is actually gaining, which contradicts the definition of sacrifice.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is false but R is true
A is true but R is false
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true and R explains A. Sacrificing ratio = Old – New. If old > new, result is positive = sacrifice. If old < new, result is negative, but then we call it a gain (gaining ratio). A sacrifice is by definition positive (you give something up). A negative would mean you're receiving more, which is gain, not sacrifice. R correctly explains this definitional constraint.

questions.question: A, B, C share profits 5:4:1. A retires. B and C share in 3:1. Goodwill = ₹2,00,000. Capitals: A=₹1,50,000, B=₹1,00,000, C=₹50,000. General Reserve = ₹1,00,000. Calculate B's total capital after all retirement adjustments.
questions.explanation: B's opening capital is Rs.1,00,000. B receives General Reserve in old ratio: 4/10 x Rs.1,00,000 = Rs.40,000. B's old share is 4/10 and new share is 3/4, so B gains 7/20. B compensates retiring partner A for goodwill by 7/20 x Rs.2,00,000 = Rs.70,000. B's final capital = Rs.1,00,000 + Rs.40,000 - Rs.70,000 = Rs.70,000.

questions.question: When the ratio of remaining partners after one retires is not mentioned, they are assumed to share the retiring partner's portion:
options: Equally between themselves
In their old ratio (i.e., old ratio relative to each other)
In ratio of their capitals
The Court decides the distribution
questions.explanation: When no agreement is made about how the retiring partner's share is divided, the presumption is that the remaining partners take the share in their OLD ratio relative to each other. This maintains their existing relative proportions. The new total is then the remaining partners' old shares plus their proportionate gain from the retiree's share.

questions.question: X, Y, Z share profits in 2:3:5. X retires; Y and Z take over in old ratio. Find new ratio of Y and Z and gaining ratio.
options: Y:Z = 3:5 (same as before); gaining ratio = 3:5 of X's share
Y new = 3/8; Z new = 5/8; gaining ratio = 3:5
Y new = 7/10; Z new = 3/10 — not possible
Both gain proportionally: Y gains 3/8×2/10; Z gains 5/8×2/10
questions.explanation: X's share = 2/10. Y and Z take in old ratio 3:5 (their relative ratio). Y gains 3/8×2/10=6/80=3/40. Z gains 5/8×2/10=10/80=1/8. Y new=3/10+3/40=12/40+3/40=15/40=3/8. Z new=5/10+1/8=4/8+1/8=5/8. New ratio Y:Z=3:5=3/8:5/8. Gaining ratio=3:5 (same as old relative ratio). Option B and A both correctly state Y:Z=3:5. Option B is more precisely expressed.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): When the new partner brings in goodwill in cash, it is distributed to the old partners in the sacrificing ratio, not the old ratio.
Reason (R): The old partners sacrifice profit share for the new partner, so they should be compensated in proportion to their individual sacrifice.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is false but R is true
A is true but R is false
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true and R explains A. When a new partner pays cash for goodwill, it is credited to the sacrificing (old) partners in their SACRIFICING ratio — because the new partner's share comes from specific sacrifices made by specific old partners. R correctly explains this: compensation should match the sacrifice made. If sacrificing ratio = old ratio, both methods give the same result.

questions.question: A, B, C share profits in 2:3:4. New partner D is admitted for 3/9 share. Old partners sacrifice in old ratio. Goodwill = ₹90,000. D pays goodwill in cash. How is goodwill distributed among A, B, C?
options: Each gets ₹30,000 equally

questions.question: A:B:C = 3:3:4. C retires. A and B share equally going forward. Goodwill on C's retirement = ₹1,60,000. C's capital = ₹80,000. Revaluation loss = ₹20,000. What is total settlement amount for C?
options: ₹80,000 + goodwill – revaluation = ₹1,36,000
All options show ₹1,36,000
questions.explanation: C's capital = ₹80,000. C's revaluation loss share (old ratio 4/10) = 4/10×20,000=₹8,000 Dr. C's goodwill = 4/10×1,60,000=₹64,000 Cr. Total C's capital = 80,000–8,000+64,000=₹1,36,000. All options A, B, C correctly compute ₹1,36,000, so Option D (all are correct) is the answer.

questions.question: Which of the following does NOT affect the total capital of the firm at the time of change in profit-sharing ratio (assuming no cash inflow or outflow)?
options: Goodwill adjustment between partners
General reserve distribution
Revaluation of assets
All of the above affect total capital
questions.explanation: Goodwill adjustment between partners (gaining Dr, sacrificing Cr) involves only transfers between capital accounts — total capital remains unchanged (one increases, another decreases by equal amount). General reserve distribution also only transfers within capitals. Revaluation similarly redistributes within capitals. Total capital (sum of all partners) does not change in any of these transactions unless cash enters or leaves. Option A is technically correct in isolation, but since all three merely redistribute, Option D is also arguable. The most precise answer: Goodwill adjustment (transfers between capitals) doesn't change total; nor does reserve distribution or revaluation (all are internal). So the question's correct answer is Option A in isolation.

questions.question: A firm's net assets excluding goodwill = ₹8,00,000. Average profit = ₹1,20,000. Normal profit rate = 10%. Goodwill valued using capitalisation of super profit method. Partners have ratio 3:2. New ratio after C's admission (1/5 share) and old partners sacrifice equally. Compute goodwill to be paid by C.
options: ₹4,00,000 (full goodwill)
₹80,000 (C's share of goodwill)
Both B and C are correct
questions.explanation: Normal profit = 10%×8,00,000=₹80,000. Super profit = 1,20,000–80,000=₹40,000. Capitalised value = Super profit/Normal rate = 40,000/0.10=₹4,00,000. This is the total goodwill of the firm. C's share = 1/5. C pays 1/5×4,00,000=₹80,000 as goodwill. Options B and C both give ₹80,000. Option D (both B and C) is correct.

questions.question: When existing goodwill is written off during reconstitution and partners write it off in old ratio, the credit appears in:
options: Profit & Loss Appropriation Account
Revaluation Account
Goodwill Account (Cr side showing write-off)
Partners' Capital Accounts (Dr)
questions.explanation: When goodwill is written off, the entry is: All Partners' Capital A/c Dr (in old or new ratio depending on procedure); Goodwill A/c Cr. The credit appears in the Goodwill Account, reducing it to zero (or new value). The debit reduces partners' capital accounts. The Goodwill Account is credited to close it, not Revaluation or P&L Appropriation.

questions.question: P, Q, R share profits 5:3:2. New ratio 4:4:2. Goodwill in books = ₹30,000. Agreed goodwill = ₹90,000. Steps: (1) Write off existing goodwill in old ratio, (2) Raise to new value, (3) Adjust. What is P's net capital change from goodwill steps?
options: P Cr Rs.15,000 net from goodwill adjustment
P Dr Rs.6,000 net from goodwill adjustment
P Cr Rs.30,000 - Dr Rs.15,000 = net Cr Rs.15,000
P net nil from these steps combined
questions.explanation: Existing goodwill of Rs.30,000 is written off in old ratio, so P is debited 5/10 x Rs.30,000 = Rs.15,000. P sacrifices 1/10 when the ratio changes from 5:3:2 to 4:4:2, so P is credited 1/10 x agreed goodwill Rs.90,000 = Rs.9,000. Net capital change for P from goodwill adjustment = Rs.6,000 debit.

questions.question: A, B, C partners (ratio 6:3:1). Firm sold. Realisation of assets = ₹4,00,000. Liabilities = ₹1,00,000. Partners' capitals before reconstitution: A=₹1,50,000, B=₹80,000, C=₹50,000. This is DISSOLUTION not ratio change. BUT assuming instead: new partner D joins for 1/10 while A, B, C sacrifice in old ratio. Goodwill = ₹1,00,000. How much does D pay as goodwill premium?
options: ₹10,000 to the firm
₹10,000 to A, B, C in ratio 6:3:1
D doesn't pay goodwill if admitted as equal partner
₹1,00,000 full goodwill
questions.explanation: D's share = 1/10. D's goodwill contribution = 1/10×1,00,000=₹10,000. This is paid to old partners A, B, C in their sacrificing ratio. Since A, B, C sacrifice in old ratio 6:3:1, the sacrificing ratio = old ratio = 6:3:1. A receives 6/10×10,000=₹6,000; B receives ₹3,000; C receives ₹1,000. Option B (₹10,000 to A, B, C in ratio 6:3:1) is correct.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): When only two partners change ratio between them and third partner's share is unchanged, only those two partners are involved in goodwill entry.
Reason (R): The third partner neither sacrifices nor gains, so there is no financial impact on their capital from the goodwill adjustment.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is false but R is true
Both A and R are false
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true and R explains A. If a third partner's ratio remains unchanged, their sacrificing and gaining ratio is both zero. Therefore, the goodwill adjustment entry is only between the two partners whose ratio changes — one debited (gaining) and one credited (sacrificing). The neutral partner's capital account is not touched. R accurately explains why A is correct.

questions.question: A and B share profits in ratio 4:1. New ratio is 2:3. Goodwill = ₹1,00,000. Additionally, General Reserve = ₹50,000. What is the total net credit to A's capital account?
options: A Cr ₹40,000 (goodwill) + Cr ₹40,000 (reserve) = ₹80,000
A Dr ₹40,000 (goodwill) + Cr ₹40,000 (reserve) = ₹0
A Cr ₹40,000 (reserve) – Dr ₹40,000 (goodwill) = net ₹0
A Cr ₹80,000 net
questions.explanation: Step 1: Gen Reserve in old ratio 4:1. A: 4/5×50,000=₹40,000 Cr. Step 2: Goodwill. Old A=4/5. New A=2/5. A sacrifices 2/5. B gains 2/5. B's Capital Dr 2/5×1,00,000=₹40,000; A's Capital Cr ₹40,000. Net A: +40,000+40,000=+₹80,000 Cr net. Options B and C show ₹0 (incorrect). Option A shows ₹80,000 Cr which is correct.

questions.question: A:B:C = 5:3:2. They change ratio to 2:3:5. Goodwill = ₹2,00,000. General Reserve = ₹50,000. Investment Fluctuation Reserve = ₹20,000 (investments at cost, market value higher). Accumulated loss = ₹15,000. Net impact on A's capital from all four adjustments?
options: A loses significantly due to goodwill Dr and loss Dr
A: Cr ₹60,000 (goodwill) + Cr ₹25,000 (reserve) + Cr ₹10,000 (IFR) – Dr ₹7,500 (loss) = Cr ₹87,500
A Dr ₹60,000 (goodwill) + Cr ₹32,500 (reserves/losses) = net Dr ₹27,500
questions.explanation: Old A=5/10. New A=2/10. A sacrifices 3/10. C gains: 2/10–5/10=–3/10 gain. C's Capital Dr 3/10×2,00,000=₹60,000; A's Capital Cr ₹60,000. Gen Reserve: A Cr 5/10×50,000=₹25,000. IFR: investments below market; no loss, so full ₹20,000 to A: 5/10×20,000=₹10,000 Cr. Accumulated loss: A Dr 5/10×15,000=₹7,500. Net A: +60,000+25,000+10,000–7,500=+₹87,500 Cr. Option C shows this exact calculation.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a situation where the old partners' capitals would need to be adjusted through cash at the time of reconstitution?
options: New partner admitted and brings in capital
Partners decide to make capitals proportional to new ratio
Goodwill adjustment between existing partners
Partner with excess capital wants to withdraw surplus
questions.explanation: Goodwill adjustment between existing partners (when changing ratio) is done by passing entries in capital accounts only — no cash changes hands between partners. It's a book entry (Dr gaining partner's capital; Cr sacrificing partner's capital). Options A (new capital), B (proportional capital via cash), and D (cash withdrawal) all involve actual cash transactions. Option C (goodwill adjustment) does not.

questions.question: A:B = 3:1. New ratio = 1:3. Goodwill = ₹1,20,000. General Reserve = ₹40,000. P&L Cr = ₹20,000. Machinery appreciation = ₹30,000. Summarise all adjustments to compute A's final capital change (starting from zero base).
options: A: Goodwill sacrifices Cr ₹60,000; rest as Cr items; total Cr ₹127,500
A: goodwill Dr ₹60,000 + Credits from reserves = net Dr ₹37,500
questions.explanation: Old A=3/4. New A=1/4. A sacrifices 2/4=1/2. B gains 1/2. B Dr ₹60,000; A Cr ₹60,000 (goodwill). Gen Reserve: A Cr 3/4×40,000=₹30,000. P&L Cr: A Cr 3/4×20,000=₹15,000. Machinery appreciation (revaluation profit): A Cr 3/4×30,000=₹22,500. Net A: +60,000+30,000+15,000+22,500=+₹1,27,500 Cr. Option D shows +₹1,27,500 which is the correct net. But the note in Option A has the direction wrong for goodwill (shows Cr not Dr on the sacrifice side) — since A sacrifices, A gets Cr from B.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): In CUET and Class 12 exams, the change in profit-sharing ratio chapter primarily tests knowledge of sacrificing ratio, gaining ratio, goodwill adjustment, and capital adjustments.
Reason (R): These four concepts form the mathematical and conceptual core of all reconstitution problems and appear in nearly every question on this topic.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is false but R is true
Both A and R are false
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true and R explains A. Sacrificing ratio, gaining ratio, goodwill adjustment, and capital adjustment are indeed the four pillars of this chapter. Every question — whether numerical, case-based, or conceptual — tests one or more of these four elements. R correctly identifies why A is true: these concepts are foundational to reconstitution and invariably appear in exam questions on this topic.

questions.question: Comprehensive Case: A, B, C share profits in 4:3:3. On 1st April 2025: (1) New ratio agreed = 3:3:4. (2) Goodwill valued at ₹3,00,000 (not in books). (3) Building appreciated by ₹60,000. (4) Stock overvalued by ₹15,000. (5) General Reserve = ₹90,000. (6) P&L Cr = ₹30,000. (7) Capitals: A=₹2,00,000; B=₹1,50,000; C=₹1,00,000. Calculate C's capital after ALL adjustments.
options: C's Capital = Rs.1,79,500
C's Capital = Rs.2,10,000
C's Capital = Rs.2,40,000
C's Capital = Rs.1,70,000
questions.explanation: C's opening capital is Rs.1,00,000. Revaluation profit = Rs.60,000 - Rs.15,000 = Rs.45,000, and C receives 3/10 x Rs.45,000 = Rs.13,500. General Reserve credit = 3/10 x Rs.90,000 = Rs.27,000. P&L credit = 3/10 x Rs.30,000 = Rs.9,000. C gains 1/10 in the new ratio, so C is credited 1/10 x Rs.3,00,000 = Rs.30,000 for goodwill. Final C capital = Rs.1,00,000 + Rs.13,500 + Rs.27,000 + Rs.9,000 + Rs.30,000 = Rs.1,79,500.
